name: Chicken Stock or Broth by James Beard, Chef
recipeInstructions:
1. Place the chicken pieces, vegetables, garlic, herbs, peppercorns, and water in a deep 8-quart pot or stockpot.
2. Bring to a boil.
3. After 5 minutes, skim off any scum that forms on the surface with a wire skimmer or large spoon.
4. Continue boiling rapidly for 15 minutes, skimming, then reduce heat; cover and simmer for 2 to 2-1/2 hours.
5. Season with salt to taste (about 1 tablespoon).
6. Strain the broth through a sieve lined with several thicknesses of cheesecloth into a large bowl and cool thoroughly in the refrigerator.
7. Save the gizzards (they are good eating) and discard the other chicken parts and vegetables.
8. When the stock is cold, remove the layer of fat that has formed on the surface.
9. You will have about 2-1/2 quarts of stock. It is a great aid and comfort to always have homemade beef, chicken, or veal stock on hand, but you must gauge your stock-making by the space available for storage. Two or three days is about as long as you should keep stock in the refrigerator; if kept longer, boil it up again before using. If you want to keep it for much longer periods of time, freeze it. You can safely keep stock frozen for up to three months.
10. Double Chicken Broth
11. Put the cold, fat-free, 2-1/2 quarts of previously made chicken stock into an 8-quart pan.
12. Add a whole stewing fowl or roasting chicken weighing 4 to 5 pounds. Bring slowly to a boil.
13. Again, skim off any scum that forms on the surface; reduce heat; cover and simmer gently until the chicken is very tender (about 1 hour for a young chicken, or 2 to 2-1/2 hours for a fowl).
14. Remove the chicken and either serve it as poached chicken or remove the skin, take the meat from the bones, and use it for chicken dishes such as salad, hash, chicken pie, or creamed chicken.
15. Strain the broth through several thicknesses of cheesecloth into a bowl; let cool, then skim off the fat.
16. You now have two quarts of beautifully rich, strong broth to use for cooking. For reducing and clarifying it further for consommé, see the recipe: Chicken Consomme by James Beard. Note: Ensure all scum is removed from the chicken stock broth and double chicken broth before straining, as well as removing all fat after cooling.
